summary: Stancil uses a bar-pricing parable to argue that pricing models are economically flexible but socially constrained by expectations. He contends that dbt’s usage-based pricing is theoretically sensible and incentive-aligned, yet the real conflict is with entrenched norms from its free/open-source roots, which will shape who it ultimately builds for.
money_quotes: We mostly accept pricing as a matter of precedent.
A social one, about pricing expectations.
If a product is already useful but people are used to getting it for free, trying to claim that value is a very delicate line to walk.
If the id of a man is in his bar, the id of a company is in its customers.
key_insight: The “right” pricing model is less about economics than expectations, and those expectations determine which customers a company will ultimately serve.
